feed.title: Combined summary - [Research] Bitcoin After Block Rewards : preventing miner's deviation when the Bitcoin rewards is zero

entry.title: Combined summary - [Research] Bitcoin After Block Rewards : preventing miner's deviation when the Bitcoin rewards is zero
entry.summary: Junhyuk Lee's research paper delves into the future implications of the security of Bitcoin as block rewards diminish, a situation anticipated to become critical by 2140. The paper addresses concerns regarding a potential decrease in miners' profitability due to the halving of block rewards and explores whether subsequent increases in Bitcoin prices could adequately compensate for these losses. Lee introduces a threshold, termed G_t, to identify deviations in miners' behavior from their expected roles within the network, suggesting that significant deviations might arise not only from changes in block rewards but also from other influencing factors. The study warns of possible deviations in a scenario where transaction fees become the sole source of revenue for miners, highlighting the risk of such deviations even when fees constitute as low as 0.17% of transaction values. Further analysis on the topic is provided through an online post discussing the implementation of a floor fee as a potential solution. This method is considered viable and compatible with soft-forking processes. The post suggests combining tail emission with an EIP-1559 style fee burn and priority tipping to address various aspects of transaction fees and network sustainability effectively. For those interested in a deeper exploration of these concepts, the original content can be accessed [here](https://delvingbitcoin.org/t/addressing-the-diminishing-block-subsidy/2640). Feedback on the paper reveals it as overly verbose, attributed to its AI-generated nature, which complicates effective engagement and comprehension. Suggestions for simplification were made to enhance its accessibility and utility. The feedback further critiques the static hash power model used in Lee's study, proposing instead a more dynamic approach that considers fluctuations in mining power and potential strategic behaviors such as entering or exiting the mining market. This critique underlines the necessity for improved observability within the network and proposes mechanisms for enhanced security, including value-scaled confirmation depth and witnessed propagation to ensure comprehensive verification of blocks before acceptance into the chain. Cameron's response to the paper highlights several critical assumptions and recommendations concerning the game theory model employed in the analysis. He critiques the realism of the model's static nature regarding mining operations and underscores the need for a more holistic and dynamic approach to modeling security within blockchain frameworks. Cameron suggests incorporating the stake of the system as a crucial factor and advocates for a stake-dependent security model that utilizes "dark capacity" or reserve mining power that can be activated as necessary, aiming for a more adaptable and efficient security model. The conversation between Junhyuk and Cameron continues with Cameron emphasizing the potential of modeling the contest of block-level double-spend races, which could escalate into bidding wars for capacity itself. He references existing models by Budish, Bonneau, and Moroz et al., which outline scenarios with and without strategic defenders, pointing out the importance of acknowledging these models as bounds for claims about network security under different assumptions. Cameron advises explicitly stating the scope limitations of Lee’s paper, ensuring clarity in its theoretical framework and assumptions.
